data.title: Fed's T-Bill Purchase Should Impact Only Very Short End of Treasury Curve — Market Talk
data.description:
1117 GMT - The Federal Reserve's decision to begin expanding its balance sheet in order to insure "ample" reserves and to buy T-bills shouldn't have any impact on the Treasury curve beyond the very short end, AllianceBernstein's Eric Winograd says in a note. The Fed will resume normal purchases of Treasury bills, aiming to buy $40 billion worth of T-bills per month for the time being. "This decision should not have any impact on markets beyond money markets and the very front end of the curve," the chief U.S. economists says. Expanding the balance sheet wasn't a surprise, though some had thought the Fed might wait until January, he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1100 GMT - U.S. Treasury yields stay lower in European midday trade, after the Federal Reserve cut interest rates on Wednesday, leaving the door open to further reductions. Some investors had positioned for a more cautious message from the Fed, says Exness' Eric Chia in a note. The Fed's plan to begin purchasing short-dated Treasury bills adds further downward pressure on yields, the financial market strategist says. Still, longer-dated Treasury yields trim some of their earlier fall. "The Fed's tone reinforced expectations for two rate cuts in 2026, while the institution's forecasts signaled one possible reduction," Chia says. Two-year Treasury yields fall 2.9 basis points to 3.535%. Ten-year yields fall 1.7bps to 4.147%, having earlier traded around 4bps lower, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1043 GMT - The U.S. dollar could face downside risks again next year, Edmond de Rothschild Asset Management's Benjamin Melman says in a webinar. "The dollar is at risk if the market frets again of U.S. rates or if we see burst of AI bubble," he says. The dollar has weakened this year as the Federal Reserve has cut interest rates. The DXY dollar index is last down 0.2% at 98.596. In mid-September, it hit a three-and-a-half-year low of 96.218. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

1008 GMT - The Turkish lira could weaken further after hitting another record low against the dollar overnight as Turkey's central bank might cut interest rates by more than expected in a decision at 1100 GMT, Commerzbank's Tatha Ghose says in a note. The median consensus forecast is for a 100 basis points rate cut but Commerzbank is leaning towards a 150bp move, citing remarks from the central bank's Governor Fatih Karahan and Finance Minister Mehmet Simsek that underlying inflation is moderating. Recent remarks from policymakers suggest "tight monetary policy has delivered its anticipated outcome," Ghose says. Commerzbank expects the dollar to rise to 43.00 lira in coming weeks. The dollar trades flat at 42.6127 lira after hitting a high of 42.6208 overnight, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1006 GMT - An interest-rate increase by the European Central Bank in 2026 looks very unlikely, Edmond de Rothschild Asset Management's Benjamin Melman says in a webinar. "We don't believe it is going to happen," he says. It would be "madness" to raise rates so soon, particularly with Germany starting a large-scale fiscal expansion program after years of a sluggish economic performance and with France's fiscal struggles, he says. Money markets aren't pricing any ECB rate hikes for 2026, according to LSEG. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0959 GMT - India's consumer inflation likely ticked up to 0.7% on year in November, according to the median estimate of nine economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. That would compare with the record low of 0.25% for October. A jump in fuel and energy inflation, which was likely boosted by "residual seasonal noise," is expected to have driven the headline CPI higher, Pantheon Macroeconomics says. Food prices likely remained subdued, while household goods and services prices likely eased further due to a recent cut in the goods and services tax rate, ANZ Research analysts write in a note. "There are no signs of demand-driven price pressures" that should worry the central bank, leaving room for more growth-centric monetary policy, they say. The data is due Friday. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0955 GMT - Mexico's market is likely becoming harder for China to access after Mexican lawmakers approved new tariffs, Capital Economics' William Jackson says in a note. Mexico has played a key role in China's postpandemic export boom, and is China's 11th-largest export market and the No.1 destination for its cars, the economist adds. A key question for China's exporters is whether other countries might do the same. Protectionist measures against China are more likely to come from countries with weaker political ties to China, larger manufacturing sectors and low reliance on Chinese demand, he says. Tariffs could raise costs of inputs in Mexico's manufacturing sector, potentially hurting its competitiveness, he adds. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0946 GMT - The Philippine central bank is expected to cut its policy rate by an additional 25 bps in 1Q, as the country's macroeconomic momentum will likely remain subdued until at least 2H next year, ANZ Research economists write in a report. The BSP lowered its rate, citing governance issues around public infrastructure projects that have weighed more heavily on growth than previously expected. "These challenges have dampened both business and consumer sentiment, constraining domestic demand," the economists say. External headwinds could further exacerbate the country's growth outlook, they add. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0925 GMT - The euro trades near a two-month high against the dollar reached overnight, and it could rise further, ING's Chris Turner says in a note. The gains reflect the Federal Reserve's interest-rate cut on Wednesday and the potential for further cuts. Meanwhile, the European Central Bank looks set to hold rates as policymakers sound optimistic about the growth outlook. The market has priced out another ECB rate cut, although it's probably "far too early" to price in 2026 rate rises, Turner says. The euro could rise to $1.1800 by year-end, although this would require weak U.S. jobs data next week or positive ECB growth forecast revisions, he says. The euro trades steady at $1.1689 after hitting $1.1707 overnight, LSEG data show. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0915 GMT - Copper prices rise after the Federal Reserve cut interest rates and as traders remain concerned over signs of tightness in the physical market. In early trading, futures on the London Metal Exchange are up 0.5% to $11,616 a metric ton, while the U.S. dollar index is down 0.2% to 98.67. A lower-interest-rate environment typically boosts economic activity and demand, and a weaker dollar makes commodities cheaper for overseas buyers. Meanwhile, the market is shrugging off China's weaker economic data and focusing instead on copper's long-term demand from renewables, EVs and data centers, ANZ analysts say. These rosy demand prospects clash with growing supply concerns amid a series of mine disruptions worldwide, keeping prices elevated. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0913 GMT - Default rates in the U.S. leveraged finance sector are expected to fall in 2026 as the Federal Reserve continues to cut interest rates, analysts at Fitch Ratings say in a note. "More accommodating monetary policy" should provide relief for highly-indebted issuers struggling with high-interest expense, the analysts say. The U.S. leveraged loan default rate will close 2026 in a range between 4.5% and 5.0%, while the U.S. high-yield default rate will be between 2.5% and 3.0%, they estimate. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - BNP Paribas takes profit on a short 10-year U.S. Treasury trade--which bet on a rise in yields--after the U.S. Federal Reserve cut interest rates as expected on Wednesday, its analysts say in a note. BNP Paribas entered the trade at 4.09% and closed it at 4.15%. "We see the Federal Reserve's imbalanced reaction function weighing on the rates markets ahead of the next payrolls data," they say. Although the Fed's vote was split, policymakers are placing emphasis on U.S. labor market's weakness. The 10-year Treasury yield falls 3.1 basis points to last trade at 4.132%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy, communication services stocks power Sri Lankan shares
data.description:
Sri Lankan shares closed higher on Thursday, led by gains in energy and communication services sectors.

  • The CSE All Share index settled 0.16% higher at 22,459.28.

  • Most sectors advanced on the day, while Industrial Asphalts and Autodrome were the top two percentage gainers on the benchmark index, rising about 25% each.

  • Trading volume on the CSE All Share index declined to 170.8 million shares from 185 million in the previous session.

  • The equity market's turnover dropped to 5.55 billion Sri Lankan rupees (about $18 million) from 6.22 billion rupees in the previous session, exchange data showed.

  • Foreign investors net sold stocks worth 210.2 million rupees, while domestic investors were net buyers, purchasing shares worth 5.49 billion rupees, the data showed.

data.title: Fitch Ratings: European LevFin Credit Conditions Ease, Risks Remain Uneven in 2026
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-London-11 December 2025: Easing funding costs and steady macroeconomic conditions should support gradual debt reduction across European leveraged finance (LevFin) in 2026, Fitch Ratings says.

Leveraged issuers in the chemicals and automotive-related sectors are the most exposed to downgrade risks.

We have revised our sector outlook for European LevFin to ‘neutral’ from ‘deteriorating’. The refinancing wave that began in 2H23 and accelerated in 2025 allowed many issuers, especially in syndicated loans, to extend maturities at reduced margins. Deleveraging will be gradual but widespread. We expect debt capital markets to remain supportive in 2026. Inflows into high-yield funds and CLO formation should sustain demand, benefiting issuers. However, there is limited room for further spread tightening.

Robust investor demand is unlikely to extend to stressed sectors and issuers, leaving them exposed to refinancing challenges. Private credit may offer solutions in some cases, but some transactions may still qualify as distressed debt exchanges (DDEs) under Fitch’s criteria.

We forecast European default rates of 3.0%-3.5% for institutional leveraged loans (LLs) and 3.75%-4.25% for high-yield bonds in 2026. Our forecast for LLs exceeds the 2.6% trailing twelve-month (TTM) October default rate, while our forecast for bonds is slightly lower than the 4.8% TTM October default rate.

We expect stable performance in most European corporate sectors in 2026; however, some will face challenges, particularly chemicals due to tariffs and excess APAC supply. Manufacturing should benefit from fiscal loosening, mainly in Germany, although high energy costs continue to create challenges. Healthcare services will remain under pressure due to complex or onerous cost structures, public budget constraints and geopolitical tensions.

We see potential negative rating actions for issuers with slow deleveraging and weak free cash flow, particularly in pressured sectors. Given highly selective investor demand, stressed low ‘B’ to ‘CCC’ credits with maturities within 24 months may find public refinancing expensive, prompting lenders to pursue aggressive strategies and recapitalisations that we may classify as DDEs.

The European private credit market is expanding in scale and sophistication, with assets under management projected to reach about EUR1 trillion by 2030, according to Preqin. The dominant strategy is direct lending to corporates, notably private placements, term loan Bs and unitranche loans. LBO and M&A financing are the primary uses of proceeds, making private credit a key funding source for mid- and small‑cap buyouts. The UK, France and Germany are the main dealmaking hubs.

We expect the credit quality in this subset of the portfolio to remain broadly unchanged in 2026. About two-thirds of our private credit portfolio is rated ‘B-’/‘b-*’. This reflects smaller company size and high leverage (especially in unitranche structures), often due to aggressive debt-funded growth expansion, as well as limited business diversification and customer concentration. About 80% of these companies have EBITDA of EUR50 million or below.

Fitch does not view private credit risks as systemic at present, despite rapid growth and financial innovation. The sector remains a small part of the financial system and is typically structured through committed-capital funds with limited redemption risk and moderate fund-level leverage. However, in a broader economic stress, private credit could become a meaningful transmission channel.

data.title: Forward prices hit one-week high on dry weather outlook, low water reserves
data.description:
Nordic forward power prices rose to a one-week high on Thursday, supported by forecasts of drier weather and lower water reserves in the hydro-power dependent region.

* The Nordic front-quarter power contract (ENOFBLQc1) rose 2.1 euro, or 3.9%, to 56.10 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) at 1041 GMT.

* The Nordic front-year contract (ENOFBLYc1) gained 0.90 euro to 37.90 euros/MWh. Both contracts rose to their highest since December 4.

* "The forecasts have turned a bit drier and less windy looking towards the last days ahead of Christmas, and given how much prices have fallen recently, this is enough to trigger a rebound," Mind Energy analysts said in a note.

* Supporting prices, the Nordic water reserves available 15 days ahead (PCAEC00) were seen at 3.15 terawatt hours above normal, compared with 6.37 terawatt hours above normal on Wednesday.

* "Towards the week after next week, rising pressure over the continent and the eastern North Atlantic will bring less active and, eventually, also cooler weather to northern Europe," said LSEG meteorologist Georg Müller.

* Germany's year-ahead power (TRDEBYc1), Europe's benchmark contract, rose 0.49 euro to 83.59 euros per MWh.

* Dutch and British wholesale gas prices edged higher as forecasts for lower wind power output increased demand for gas from gas-fired power plants, but prices remained close to 20-month lows.

* In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) rose 1.5% to 83.64 euros a tonne.

* Meanwhile, the Nordic power price for next-day physical delivery (FXSYSAL=NPX), or system price, gained 3.83 euros to 52.05 euros per MWh.

data.title: REG - Rome Resources PLC - Board Changes
data.description:
RNS Number : 2237L Rome Resources PLC 11 December 2025  

11 December 2025

Rome Resources plc

("Rome Resources", the "Company" or the "Group")

Board Changes

Rome Resources (AIM: RMR), the DRC-focused tin and copper explorer, announces that Mr. Edouard Etienvre and Mr. Marc Mathenz have resigned as non-executive directors of the Company with immediate effect.

On 3 September 2025, the Company announced the proposed appointment of Stephane Mutombo Irung as a non-executive director to the board of directors of Rome (the "Board" or the "Directors"). In addition to the proposed appointment of Mr. Irung, the process to recruit an additional non-executive director to the Board has commenced and a further update will be issued when appropriate.

Under the AIM Rules for Companies and AIM Rules for Nominated Advisers, the Company's Nominated Adviser is required to undertake a director due diligence process to satisfy itself as to board composition, independence, suitability and the efficacy of the board as a whole in relation to any potential board changes for the purposes of the AIM Rules for Nominated Advisers, with a view to determining that the Company is suitable to be a Company with shares admitted to trading on AIM. Any appointment to the Board is subject to the approval from the Company's nominated adviser.

Mr. Etienvre and Mr. Mathenz are to be paid the sums due to them under their contractual notice periods and will retain the warrants over new ordinary shares previously granted to them.  Mr. Etienvre and Mr. Mathenz will continue to benefit from the Company's directors' and officers' insurance policy for a period of five years hereon and have also agreed, among other things, a mutual waiver of claims with the Company. 

Paul Barrett, Chief Executive Officer of Rome Resources, commented:

"I would like to express my gratitude to Mr. Etienvre and Mr. Mathenz for their efforts in helping steer the Company through the complex reverse takeover process last year, for their strategic advice, and their support for the Company's activities through 2025."

data.title: Serbia Central Bank Keeps Interest Rate at 5.75%
data.description:
The National Bank of Serbia left its benchmark interest rate unchanged at 5.75% at its final meeting of 2025, alongside maintaining the deposit rate at 4.5% and the credit facility rate at 7.0%.

Following a decline in inflation to 2.9% in September and 2.8% in October, the Executive Board expects it to hover around the 3% target until March next year.

GDP growth held steady at 2% in the third quarter, in line with the central bank’s expectations, driven mainly by the services and industrial sectors, while construction activity continued to contract.

The Board projects economic growth of 2.1% for 2025, accelerating to 3.5% in 2026, supported by household consumption and fixed investment.

The Board reiterated a cautious stance amid global uncertainty, trade tensions, and geopolitical risks, stressing its commitment to a data-driven and flexible policy approach aimed at preserving financial stability and supporting growth.

data.title: Turkish Central Bank Cuts Rates Again
data.description:
By Don Nico Forbes

Turkey's central bank cut interest rates again at its latest meeting, after inflation cooled more than expected in November.

The Central Bank of Turkey cut its benchmark rate to 38.0% from 39.5% on Thursday. Money markets priced in a 1.75 percentage point rate cut, although with the possibility of a bigger, 2 percentage-point cut, according to LSEG data.

data.title: WHO reaffirms no link between vaccines and autism
data.description:
The World Health Organization's vaccine safety committee said on Thursday that new reviews of scientific evidence found no link between vaccines and autism spectrum disorder, reaffirming conclusions reached more than two decades ago.

The WHO Global Advisory Committee on Vaccine Safety assessed two systematic reviews covering studies published between 2010 and August 2025.

The reviews examined vaccines in general and those containing thiomersal, a mercury-based preservative that has long been accused by critics of contributing to autism - a claim repeatedly dismissed by scientific studies.

A causal link between vaccines and health outcomes is considered only when several high-quality studies consistently show a statistical association, the committee said.

Twenty out of 31 studies found no evidence of an association between vaccines and autism, according to the WHO.

Eleven studies that suggested a possible link were deemed to have major methodological flaws and a high risk of bias, the committee said.

Last month, U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said in an interview with the New York Times that he had personally instructed the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention to change its long-standing position that vaccines do not cause autism.

data.title: Hooker Furnishings Reports Fiscal 2026 Third Quarter Results
data.description:
Hooker Furnishings Corporation, a global leader in home furnishings, has released its fiscal 2026 third quarter results, highlighting significant strategic shifts and financial performance metrics. The company has announced a strategic divestiture of value-priced home furnishings brands Pulaski Furniture and Samuel Lawrence Furniture, which are now reported as discontinued operations.

Financial Highlights

For the fiscal 2026 third quarter, Hooker Furnishings reported a consolidated net sales decrease of 14.4% year-over-year, primarily due to a reduction in shipments from the Samuel Lawrence Hospitality segment. The company recorded a net loss of $21.2 million for the quarter, driven by non-cash impairment charges and restructuring costs. Key financial metrics include:

  • Net sales: $70.7 million, down from $82.7 million in the prior year.
  • Gross profit: $18.1 million, compared to $20.5 million in the previous year.
  • Operating loss: $16.3 million, primarily due to $15.6 million in non-cash intangible impairment charges.
  • Net loss: $21.2 million, compared to a loss of $4.1 million in the prior year.
  • Basic loss per share: $(1.99), compared to $(0.39) in the previous year.

Business and Operational Highlights

Despite the overall decline in net sales, the Hooker Branded and Domestic Upholstery segments experienced modest sales growth. The company also highlighted the successful launch of the Margaritaville licensed collection, which has garnered significant retailer commitments and is expected to drive incremental revenue in the coming quarters.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Hooker Furnishings has implemented a multi-phase cost-reduction program, achieving approximately $25–$26.5 million in annualized savings. The company also announced a new shareholder return program, including share repurchases and a recalibrated dividend. The Board of Directors has authorized a new share repurchase program of up to $5 million and reduced the annual dividend by 50% to $0.46 per share.

Management's Perspective

CEO Jeremy Hoff emphasized the company's strategic focus on higher-margin, design-led brands and the successful execution of cost-reduction initiatives. CFO Earl Armstrong noted that the non-cash impairment charges do not affect the company's liquidity or ongoing operations, and the new capital allocation strategy aims to enhance long-term shareholder value.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, Hooker Furnishings is optimistic about its streamlined portfolio and cost structure, which positions the company for improved profitability even amid macroeconomic challenges. The company expects the Margaritaville collection to drive significant revenue growth in the second half of the next fiscal year.

data.title: Swiss 10-Year Bond Yield Holds Around 2-Month High
data.description:
The Swiss 10-year government bond yield was little changed at 0.28%, staying near its highest level since October 8, as investors digested the latest policy decision by the Swiss National Bank.

The central bank kept its policy rate unchanged at 0%, as expected, following a pause at its September meeting after six consecutive cuts.

The SNB said that while recent inflation has slightly undershot expectations, its primary focus remains on the medium-term outlook, which is largely unchanged.

It also expressed slightly more optimism about the economic outlook, particularly after the Swiss government secured an improved trade deal with the US. SNB Chairman Martin Schlegel reiterated that the bar for moving rates into negative territory is higher than for lowering borrowing costs when rates are positive.

Elsewhere, the US Federal Reserve delivered a widely expected rate cut and signaled that it may leave rates unchanged in the coming months.

data.title: Israel Trade Deficit Widens in November
data.description:
Israel’s trade deficit widened to USD 3.25 billion in November 2025, up from USD 2.77 billion a year earlier, as imports grew faster than exports.

Imports surged 11.6% to USD 8.37 billion, while exports rose 8.2% to USD 5.13 billion, supported by the electronic components and board manufacturing sector.

Over the first eleven months of 2025, the trade gap expanded to USD 35.31 billion, compared with USD 29.90 billion during the same period in 2024.

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

The Trump administration is locked in an intense battle with its European allies over its vision for rewiring the economies of Russia and Ukraine — a plan that includes U.S. financial firms and businesses tapping roughly $200 billion of frozen Russian assets for projects in Ukraine. Wherever the negotiations land, the outcome could completely alter Europe's economy. Separately, President Trump wants lower interest rates and a new Fed chair. But yesterday's fractured Fed vote suggests cutting rates will be harder than it looks, no matter who is in charge.

Today's Headlines

Jerome Powell pushed through a rate cut over the broadest reservations of his nearly eight-year tenure, implicitly delivering a pointed message to Trump and his own successor: Cutting rates is harder than it looks.

Venezuelan opposition leader María Corina Machado slipped through 10 military checkpoints to reach Norway to receive the Nobel Peace Prize that she won for challenging authoritarian leader Nicolás Maduro.

POWs from among 30 nationalities held in Ukraine, many of whom say they were tricked into fighting for Russia, say they plan to go to Russia rather than return home if U.S.-led peace talks lead to a full prisoner exchange.

Powell said Fed staffers believe that federal data could be overstating job creation by up to 60,000 jobs a month.

The U.S. seized an oil tanker off the coast of Venezuela, marking a major escalation in the Trump administration's pressure campaign against Maduro.

Live From The Markets

Oracle's stock has plunged 32% over the past three months, showing the growing anxiety investors have as the company's fate hinges closely on OpenAI, which still accounts for most of its revenue backlog, writes Dan Gallagher.

Coca-Cola said Chief Executive James Quincey will step down in March and be succeeded by Henrique Braun, the company's chief operating officer.

Read It Here First

A U.S. blueprint to rewire the economies of Russia and Ukraine has set off a clash with Europe.

The Trump administration has handed its European counterparts documents detailing its vision for the reconstruction of Ukraine and the return of Russia to the global economy. The U.S. blueprint has been spelled out in appendices to current peace proposals that aren't public but were described to the Journal by U.S. and European officials. One appendix details America's plan to integrate Russia's economy, with U.S. companies investing in strategic sectors like rare-earth extraction and oil drilling in the Arctic. Some European officials aren't sure whether to take some of the U.S. proposals seriously, report Joe Parkinson, Benoit Faucon and Drew Hinshaw.

China is making an AI power play using cheap electricity from the world's biggest grid.

The U.S. invented the most powerful artificial-intelligence models and controls access to the most advanced computer chips, but China has its own ace to play in the global AI contest: the biggest power grid. In China, inexpensive power has helped AI companies develop high-quality models more cheaply than U.S. competitors. It also has helped China overcome challenges posed by its inferior domestic chips. The U.S.-China "electron gap," as OpenAI calls it, has become a major preoccupation for American tech leaders, report Raffaele Huang and Brian Spegele.

Expert Take

Q: Why are so many modern jobs becoming harder to explain?

More Americans have jobs that didn't exist a generation ago, and even well-known professions are changing. Columnist Callum Borchers explains why new titles aren't exactly self-explanatory — and jobs are getting more niche.

A: For one thing, a lot of today's jobs didn't exist a short time ago. LinkedIn estimates one in five Americans has a job that was created after 2000. Many titles include technical terms that are not universally understood.

An even bigger reason why so many jobs are hard to explain might be the changing nature of work: Jobs are getting more specialized as technology automates basic tasks, and the fruits of our labor are increasingly abstract.

Consider a well-known field like accounting. I recently met a CPA who spends three-quarters of his time meeting with prospective clients and much of the rest talking to those he already has. He doesn't prepare tax returns himself but says clients prize human interaction.

This is just one example of a job where the output is mostly intangible — client sentiment is harder to quantify than a pile of completed tax returns — and the day-to-day duties are not what we tend to assume.

Then there are the millions of people trying to hack it as social-media influencers. Their work is highly visible, but the economics are fuzzy. Influencers tell me their friends and family often don't get how having lots of online followers can pay the bills.

See The Story

Trump is wielding one of the most sweeping of presidential powers — clemency — far differently in his second term than in his first. Half of his 238 first-term pardons (wiping out charges or a conviction) and commutations (reducing sentences) were given on the final day of that term in 2021. In 2025 alone, Trump has already granted clemency to 1,600 people. See what these pardons have in common, in graphics and photos.

Happening Today

The Senate is expected to vote on a Democrat-backed measure to extend enhanced healthcare subsidies.

Disgraced cryptocurrency tycoon Do Kwon is scheduled to be sentenced for fraud in connection with the $40 billion crash of his TerraUSD and Luna coins.

Economic data: Jobless claims

Earnings: Broadcom, Ciena, Costco, Lululemon

Number Of The Day:

$300,000

The cost of the apartment renovation in Paris that collector Claire Durand-Ruel undertook to create a "calm backdrop" for her extensive art collection. The transformation of the 1,300-square-foot space included turning a kitchen into a central cube and mounting a painting in the main bathroom's shower.

And Finally...

They fall asleep to the soothing sounds of a man talking about murders.

Forget counting sheep: A subset of true-crime fans finds that the best way to fall asleep is by listening to the classic docuseries "Forensic Files." These viewers — like 43-year-old Dennis Long — find narrator Peter Thomas's voice both engaging and anesthetizing. Long says he is "out like a light" less than 30 minutes after turning the show on. Fans who worried they were alone in adding "Forensic Files" to their nighttime routine have found communities of like-minded people on Facebook and Reddit.

data.title: Germany - BESS boom problems
data.description:
Germany has received over 500GW of connection requests for battery storage projects, an increase on 300GW at the start of the year, pointing to major grid challenges for the country and cannibalisation of price volatility due to increasing competition, according to analysis by Wood Mackenzie.

Germany is Europe's largest power system, but faces a capacity crunch with significant implications for grid operators and investors, analysts found. They said that residential BESS storage projects cannot adequately address issues of security in supply caused by the phase out of nuclear, a decline in coal capacity of 29GW by 2030 and a struggling gas sector, with a capacity market on the horizon.

BESS projects in Germany are competing for volatility at peak price margins, putting pressure on revenue streams. “Frequency market revenues, highly sought after in Germany, represent shallow revenue pools typically well below 1% of system peak demand in GW terms. They quickly saturate as BESS deployments approach and overtake this market need,” the analysts said.

Rory McCarthy, head of power and renewables consulting EMEA at Wood Mackenzie, said: “The key question becomes: what optimisation strategies can deliver revenues above the mean to achieve target returns in an increasingly competitive landscape?"

Elsewhere in Europe, Wood Mackenzie found that considerable volumes are being deployed across the continent, totalling 11GW as of 2024, which is expected to grow 45% year-over-year to 16GW in 2025.

The research showed that European BESS deployment grew at a 9% compound annual growth rate over a ten-year period between 2024 and 2034.

data.title: Starton, Clinical-Stage Biotech Developing Continuous Drug Delivery Therapies for Cancer Patients, Files for NASDAQ Capital Market IPO
data.description:
Starton filed an S-1 for an initial public offering on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol “STA.” The clinical-stage biotech plans to offer 6,666,667 shares at an estimated price range of $5.00 to $7.00, led by Revere Securities LLC. Proceeds will fund clinical development of its lead program STAR-LLD in multiple myeloma and CLL, academic IIT studies, and working capital.

Business Description

Starton is a clinical‑stage biotechnology company developing continuous delivery formulations of FDA‑approved active ingredients to improve tolerability, adherence, and outcomes in oncology. The company’s platform spans three modalities—continuous subcutaneous infusion, oral controlled‑release tablets/capsules, and transdermal patches—designed to lower Cmax, reduce overall exposure (AUC), and maintain steady therapeutic levels.

The lead program, STAR‑LLD, reformulates lenalidomide (the active ingredient in Revlimid) for continuous delivery across subcutaneous, oral controlled‑release, and transdermal approaches. Initial indications focus on relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma (RRMM), chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), and combinations with CAR‑T therapies in MM and lymphoma. A Phase 1b MM study (n=6) met its primary safety objective, and a Phase 2a RRMM study and a Phase 1 CLL study are planned. The company intends to leverage the 505(b)(2) regulatory pathway and has additional technology assets (e.g., STAR‑OLZ transdermal olanzapine for CINV, currently on hold). Starton reports patent coverage extending to at least 2040 for key assets and technologies.

Market Overview

  • Total addressable market: Revlimid (lenalidomide) generated $12.8 billion globally in 2022, underscoring demand in hematologic malignancies.
  • Market growth: Continued uptake of targeted and cellular therapies in MM and CLL with opportunities for improved tolerability and maintenance strategies.
  • Market position: Starton targets superiority or new indications via continuous delivery, aiming to improve therapeutic index and adherence.
  • Key competitors: Large oncology drug developers (e.g., Bristol Myers Squibb) and companies advancing MM/CLL regimens and drug delivery technologies.
  • Industry trends: Increased interest in 505(b)(2) pathways, long-acting/controlled-release formulations, and combinations with CAR-T therapies.

Operational Metrics

  • Customers: No commercial customers; clinical‑stage programs only
  • Locations: Headquarters and R&D/GMP Phase 1 facility in Paramus, New Jersey
  • Geographic presence: Primary operations in the United States; out‑licensing in China for STAR‑OLZ
  • Partnerships: Haisco (exclusive license for STAR‑OLZ in Mainland China); Bend Biosciences engaged for OCR development; CRO partnerships in place
  • Orders/GMV: Not applicable (pre‑revenue)
  • Other key metrics: 7 full‑time employees (as of Dec 1, 2025); 9,075 sq ft facility including labs and GMP Phase 1 manufacturing; completed Phase 1 healthy volunteer BA study and a 6‑patient Phase 1b MM study; planned Phase 2a RRMM and Phase 1 CLL studies

Financials Highlights

  • Revenue (current): No revenue
  • Operating income: Operating loss of $14.3 million (FY 2025)
  • Net income: Net loss of $9.48 million (FY 2025); net loss of $14.99 million (FY 2024)

Management

  • Pedro Lichtinger Waisman, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer - Former CEO of Asterias Biotherapeutics and Optimer Pharmaceuticals; ex‑President at Pfizer business units; MBA Wharton.
  • James C. Oliver, Pharm.D., Chief Medical Officer - 30+ years in CRO and biotech; prepared 50+ INDs and participated in 7 NDAs; prior leadership roles at global CROs.
  • Scott L. Kahn, CPA, Chief Financial Officer - 35+ years in finance and accounting; prior CFO/VP roles at med‑tech and public companies; CPA (NY).

IPO Structure

  • Issuer: Starton Holdings, Inc.
  • Filing date: December 10, 2025
  • Proposed ticker: STA
  • Exchange: NASDAQ Capital Market
  • Price range: $5.00–$7.00
  • Offering size: Approximately $40 million at the midpoint, plus a 1,000,000-share over-allotment option
  • Shares offered: 6,666,667 shares (with a 1,000,000-share over-allotment option)
  • Lead underwriters: Revere Securities LLC (representative)
  • Use of proceeds: Fund a Phase 2 multiple myeloma trial and a Phase 1 CLL study for STAR-LLD, support IITs in CAR-T combinations, pay certain trade payables and related party amounts, and provide at least one year of working capital.

Original SEC Filing: Starton Holdings, Inc. - S-1 - Dec. 10, 2025
